prompt.task: Classify code review comments into categories and assign priority levels. Use the exact format demonstrated in the examples.
prompt.context: Code review comments come from a team reviewing pull requests. Comments need to be classified so they can be routed appropriately: - Blocking issues must be fixed before merge - Suggestions are optional improvements - Questions need author response - Praise requires no action

constraints.constraint: Use only the categories shown in examples: blocking, suggestion, question, praise, nitpick
Priority must be: critical, high, medium, low, none
If a comment contains multiple intents, classify by the most important one
Output valid JSON only, no markdown fences

example.input: This SQL query is vulnerable to injection. You're concatenating user input directly into the query string. Use parameterized queries instead.
example.output: { "category": "blocking", "priority": "critical", "requires_change": true, "requires_response": false, "summary": "SQL injection vulnerability - must use parameterized queries" }
example.annotation: Security issues are always blocking and critical priority. No response needed because the fix is clear.

example.input: Consider using a Map here instead of an array with .find(). It would be O(1) lookup instead of O(n). Not critical since the array is small, but good practice.
example.output: { "category": "suggestion", "priority": "low", "requires_change": false, "requires_response": false, "summary": "Performance improvement - use Map for O(1) lookup" }
example.annotation: Reviewer explicitly says "not critical" - this is a suggestion. The "good practice" phrasing indicates educational intent, not requirement.

example.input: Why did you choose to make this call synchronous? Is there a reason we can't use async/await here? I might be missing some context.
example.output: { "category": "question", "priority": "medium", "requires_change": false, "requires_response": true, "summary": "Clarification needed on synchronous vs async design choice" }
example.annotation: Questions always require response. Priority is medium because the answer might reveal a blocking issue, or might be fine.

example.input: Nice refactor! This is much cleaner than the previous implementation. Love how you extracted the validation logic.
example.output: { "category": "praise", "priority": "none", "requires_change": false, "requires_response": false, "summary": "Positive feedback on refactoring quality" }

example.description: Mixed comment - question that implies a blocking issue
example.input: Is this handling the case where the user token is expired? I don't see any expiration check, and if we ship this, users will get cryptic errors when their session times out.
example.output: { "category": "blocking", "priority": "high", "requires_change": true, "requires_response": true, "summary": "Missing token expiration handling - may cause user-facing errors" }
example.annotation: Although phrased as a question, this implies missing functionality that would cause user-facing bugs. Classify as blocking, not question. Still requires response to confirm the issue exists.

example.description: Nitpick disguised as blocking - don't over-prioritize
example.input: You should use const instead of let here since you never reassign this variable.
example.wrong_output: { "category": "blocking", "priority": "high", "requires_change": true, "requires_response": false, "summary": "Must use const instead of let" }
example.correct_output: { "category": "nitpick", "priority": "low", "requires_change": false, "requires_response": false, "summary": "Style preference - const vs let" }
example.annotation: const vs let is a stylistic preference, not a functional issue. This is a nitpick - the code works correctly either way. Don't classify style preferences as blocking issues.

prompt.input: I noticed this endpoint doesn't have any rate limiting. If someone spams this with requests, they could overload the database. We should add rate limiting before this goes to production.
prompt.output_format: Return a single JSON object matching the structure in the examples. No markdown, no explanation, just the JSON.

checks.check: Category is one of: blocking, suggestion, question, praise, nitpick
Priority is one of: critical, high, medium, low, none
